sections.markdown:
# LOCAL-TIME: Real Date/Time Handling

Common Lisp ships with `get-universal-time` (seconds since 1900) and `encode-universal-time`. They work but are spartan: no timezones, no parsing, no arithmetic on durations. `local-time` is the de facto replacement.

The central type is `timestamp`, holding a UTC instant with sub-second precision plus an associated timezone for display.

Key functions:

* `(local-time:encode-timestamp NSEC SEC MIN HOUR DAY MONTH YEAR &key timezone)` — build a deterministic timestamp.
* `(local-time:now)` — current instant. *Don't* use in tests; non-deterministic.
* `(local-time:timestamp-to-unix TS)` — seconds since 1970-01-01 UTC.
* `(local-time:unix-to-timestamp N)` — inverse.
* `(local-time:timestamp-year TS)`, `-month`, `-day`, `-hour`, etc.
* `(local-time:timestamp+ TS AMOUNT UNIT)` — add e.g. `(:day 7)`. Returns a new timestamp.
* `(local-time:format-timestring STREAM TS &key format)` — render. With `STREAM = nil`, returns a string.

For tests, always start from `encode-timestamp` so the value is reproducible across machines and runs.

sections.explain: 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC is unix epoch 0. Serves as a sanity check that ENCODE-TIMESTAMP and TIMESTAMP-TO-UNIX agree.
Field accessors return the integer for the requested component.
Add 5 days to 2000-01-01; the day-of-month is now 6.
FORMAT-TIMESTRING accepts a format spec — a list of literals and field codes. Here we render an ISO date.

sections.prompt: Define UNIX-OF (Y M D) returning the Unix epoch second of midnight UTC on Y-M-D using LOCAL-TIME. ENCODE-TIMESTAMP order is (nsec sec min hour day month year).
